one_line_takeaway: Unifying retrievers, rerankers, and reward models under a single 'text scoring' adversarial-robustness lens, the paper shows that standard PGD/HotFlip adversarial training (the current norm for LLM robustness) transfers poorly to content-injection attacks, that a specifically-introduced content-injection training method closes that gap, and that combining Rudimentary+HotFlip+PGD+Injection training yields the broadest robustness while also reducing measured reward hacking in real RLHF runs.
key_finding: From the paper's own Table 1 and Table 2: (1) Rudimentary and HotFlip training give consistent, statistically significant robustness gains against swapping/perturbation attacks (rudimentary, HotFlip, MLM swaps) across all three model roles; PGD shows a similar but weaker pattern. (2) Critical robustness-transfer gap: PGD and HotFlip/GCG-style training -- 'the current standard for LLM robustness' per the authors -- do not reliably transfer to content-injection robustness, and sometimes correlate negatively with it (e.g., reranker sentence injection under Rudimentary/HotFlip training). Content-injection training is the clearest way to reduce injection ASR (e.g., retriever sentence-injection ASR drops from 31.2% base to 10.2% with Injection training and 10.4% with Combined; reranker sentence-injection ASR drops from 21.1% base to 0.20% with Injection and 0.32% with Combined; reward-model sentence-injection ASR drops from 2.33% base to 0.03% with Injection or Combined) but transfers less well back to rudimentary/HotFlip/MLM robustness, except notably for reward models where injection training transfers more strongly to swapping/perturbation robustness than PGD does. (3) Paraphrase training gives weak/inconsistent robustness gains and is excluded from the Combined method for that reason. (4) Combined training (Rudimentary+HotFlip+PGD+Injection) is generally the most robust overall (e.g., retriever Rudimentary-attack ASR falls from 99.7% base to 93.5% Combined, average steps-to-success rising from 62.7 to 163; reward-model Rudimentary ASR falls from 93.3% base to 62.7% Combined, HotFlip ASR from 95.3% to 64.7%) while frequently also improving or preserving task effectiveness (e.g., retriever average effectiveness 57.0 base -> 57.7 Combined; reranker 61.5 -> 62.4) and lowering clean dev loss in most cases -- though specialized single-attack training sometimes still beats Combined on its own targeted attack (e.g., HotFlip-trained reranker is more robust to HotFlip than Combined; Injection-trained retrievers/rerankers edge out Combined on sentence injection). (5) In RLHF, policies trained with Combined-adversarially-trained reward models (medium and high strength) are statistically-significantly preferred over policies trained with the base (non-adversarial) reward model in pairwise LLM-judge comparisons on Arena-Hard v2 and WildBench v2 (e.g., for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct at beta=0.01, Comb-AT High vs Base RM: win/tie/loss 46.5%/11.0%/42.5%, average score +0.07, marked significant), and Combined-trained policies show lower KL-divergence per token during training (base > combined-medium > combined-high), which the authors interpret as evidence of reduced reward hacking. No adversarial training method achieves perfect robustness against any attack studied -- the paper explicitly states this as a limitation of the field, not a result unique to their methods.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is fourfold: (1) it proposes unifying the study of adversarial robustness and adversarial training across three 'text scoring' model roles -- dense retrievers, pointwise rerankers, and reward models -- arguing that text scoring gives a content-agnostic, structurally testable failure condition (an irrelevant/rejected text should never outscore a relevant/chosen one); (2) it adapts and systematically studies five adversarial training formulations for these scoring models (Rudimentary string-level perturbation training, HotFlip gradient-guided token-swap training, PGD continuous-embedding-perturbation training, a novel content-injection training method targeting sentence/query injection -- which the authors state is previously unaddressed for these model types -- and paraphrase-consistency training), plus a Combined strategy (Rudimentary + HotFlip + PGD + Injection); (3) it provides what it calls the first evaluation of adversarial-training robustness transfer across attack families and model roles for text scoring models, using Spearman correlation between training strength and robustness (Table 1) and full ASR/effectiveness tables (Table 2); (4) it applies the resulting adversarially trained reward models inside actual RLHF runs (REINFORCE++-baseline via OpenRLHF) and shows the trained policies are preferred by an LLM judge over policies trained with a non-adversarially-trained base reward model, with lower KL divergence per token, which the authors interpret as reduced reward hacking.
threat_model: The paper defines adversaries as agents that manipulate candidate texts to spuriously increase a text scoring model's score. Two attack families are studied. (1) Search-based perturbation attacks edit an existing irrelevant passage (for retrievers/rerankers) or a rejected response (for reward models) via local string/token edits: Rudimentary (character insertions/deletions/swaps, word duplications/deletions/swaps), HotFlip-guided token swaps (white-box, requires model-parameter access, gradient-guided), and MLM-guided word/token swaps (contextually plausible replacements via ModernBERT-large as a black-box-style attack that does not require gradient access to the target). Success = the edited/irrelevant candidate ranks first (retrievers/rerankers) or outscores the chosen response (reward models), found via beam search (16 beams x 16 candidates = 256 candidates/step, up to 512 steps). (2) Content injection attacks insert new text rather than editing existing text: sentence injection (an unrelated sentence inserted at start/middle/end, or LLM-generated injections) and query injection (inserting the query itself into an irrelevant passage, applicable to retrievers/rerankers only). For sentence injection, success = any score increase after injecting into a genuinely relevant/chosen text; for query injection, success = the injected passage ranking first. In RLHF, the paper additionally frames the policy model itself as an adaptive adversary that optimizes against the reward model during training, exploiting its limited robustness (i.e., reward hacking) -- this is evaluated empirically rather than via an explicit attack algorithm.
limitations: Stated explicitly in the paper: (1) No method achieves perfect robustness for any attack -- robustness gains are partial and often traded off against transfer to other attack families. (2) High attack success rates under large attack budgets (up to 512 beam-search steps) should not be read as adversarial training being ineffective; the paper notes in its Appendix G that smaller attack budgets make ASR look artificially low, implying their headline ASR numbers are pessimistic/thorough by design but also that budget choice strongly affects apparent robustness. (3) For reward models, the effectiveness trend from adversarial training is 'less clear' than for retrievers/rerankers, and the authors explicitly flag known challenges in reward-model benchmark evaluation (citing Wen et al., 2024) as a caveat on their own effectiveness numbers. (4) At high adversarial training strength, dev loss for the reward model becomes worse than the base model's dev loss, yet RLHF policies trained with it still perform comparably to medium strength -- the authors note this suggests robustness benefits extend beyond what static effectiveness/dev-loss metrics capture, but this is presented as an open question rather than a fully resolved finding. (5) HotFlip is explicitly white-box, requiring access to model parameters, which the authors flag as a methodological constraint distinguishing it from the MLM-guided attack. (6) Paraphrase training is dropped from the Combined method due to weak/inconsistent gains, meaning the paper's own 'best' recipe is empirically curated rather than a first-principles union of all studied methods.

datasets_used: RLHN training sets (MSMARCO, HotpotQA, NQ, Fever subsets) for retriever/reranker training
Skywork Reward Data Collection v0.2 for reward model training
TREC-DL19 and TREC-DL20 for retriever/reranker evaluation (NDCG@10)
Full 8.8M-passage MSMARCO corpus as the retrieval corpus for retriever evaluation
BEIR subset for retriever/reranker effectiveness evaluation
RewardBench 2 (100 filtered prompts) for reward model robustness evaluation
PPE human preference subset for reward model effectiveness evaluation
WildBench v2 (1024 prompts) and Arena-Hard v2 (750 prompts) for RLHF-policy pairwise LLM-judge evaluation
MSMARCO passage corpus and a November 2023 English Wikipedia dump as sources of random sentences for sentence-injection attacks
quantitative_results: Retriever base sentence-injection ASR 31.2% -> 10.2% with Injection training -> 10.4% with Combined training
Reranker base sentence-injection ASR 21.1% -> 0.20% with Injection training -> 0.32% with Combined training
Reward model base sentence-injection ASR 2.33% -> 0.03% with Injection or Combined training
Retriever base Rudimentary-attack ASR 99.7% (avg 62.7 steps) -> Combined 93.5% ASR (avg 163 steps)
Reward model base Rudimentary-attack ASR 93.3% -> Combined 62.7%; base HotFlip ASR 95.3% -> Combined 64.7%
Retriever average effectiveness: Base 57.0 -> Combined 57.7 (NDCG@10-based composite in Table 2)
RLHF: Comb-AT (High) reward model vs Base RM policy, beta=0.01: win/tie/loss 46.5%/11.0%/42.5%, average judge-score margin +0.07 (statistically significant)
RLHF: Comb-AT (Med) reward model vs Base RM policy, beta=0.02: win/tie/loss 46.1%/14.0%/39.9%, average judge-score margin +0.10 (statistically significant)
baselines_compared: Base (no adversarial training) retriever, reranker, and reward model
